* [Beat music](/wiki/Beat_music "Beat music") * [Brill Building](/wiki/Brill_Building_(genre) "Brill Building (genre)") * [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco") * [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk") * [hard bop](/wiki/Hard_bop "Hard bop") * [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") * [mod revival](/wiki/Mod_revival "Mod revival") * [post-disco](/wiki/Post-disco "Post-disco") * [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll") * [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") * [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") * [smooth jazz](/wiki/Smooth_jazz "Smooth jazz") * [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") * [zouk love](/wiki/Zouk_love_(music_style) "Zouk love (music style)") * [zydeco](/wiki/Zydeco "Zydeco")

or **R'n'B**, is a genre of [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") that originated within [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American") communities in the 1940s. The term was originally used by record companies to describe recordings marketed predominantly to African Americans, at a time when "rocking, [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") based music ... [with a] heavy, insistent beat" was starting to become more popular.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Music genre originating in the 1940s in the United States

This article is about the original form of R&B. For the modern form, mixed with other genres, see [Contemporary R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B").

For other uses, see [Rhythm and blues (disambiguation)](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues_(disambiguation) "Rhythm and blues (disambiguation)").

"RnB" redirects here. For the Japanese television station that uses the abbreviation RNB, see [Nankai Broadcasting](/wiki/Nankai_Broadcasting "Nankai Broadcasting"). For the EP by Ruger and Bnxn, see [RnB (EP)](/wiki/RnB_(EP) "RnB (EP)").

**Rhythm and blues**, frequently abbreviated as **R&B** or **R'n'B**, is a genre of [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") that originated within [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American") communities in the 1940s. The term was originally used by record companies to describe recordings marketed predominantly to African Americans, at a time when "rocking, [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") based music ... [with a] heavy, insistent beat" was starting to become more popular.
In the commercial rhythm and blues music typical of the 1950s through the 1970s, the bands usually consisted of a piano, one or two guitars, bass, drums, one or more saxophones, and sometimes background vocalists. R&B lyrical themes often encapsulate the African American history and experience of pain and the quest for [freedom](/wiki/Freedom "Freedom") and joy, as well as triumphs and failures in terms of societal racism, [oppression](/wiki/Oppression "Oppression"), relationships, economics, and aspirations.

The term "rhythm and blues" has undergone a number of shifts in meaning. In the early 1950s, it was frequently applied to [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") records. Starting in the mid-1950s, after this style of music had contributed to the development of [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll"), the term "R&B" became used in a wider context. It referred to music styles that developed from and incorporated [electric blues](/wiki/Electric_blues "Electric blues"), as well as [gospel](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music") and [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"). By the 1970s, the term "rhythm and blues" had changed once again and was used as a blanket term for soul and [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk").

In the late 1980s, a newer style of R&B developed, becoming known as "[contemporary R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B")". This contemporary form combines rhythm and blues with various elements of [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), soul, funk, [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco"), [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") and [electronic](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music").

## Etymology, definitions and description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhythm_and_blues&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology, definitions and description")]

Although [Jerry Wexler](/wiki/Jerry_Wexler "Jerry Wexler") of *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* magazine is credited with coining the term "rhythm and blues" as a musical term in the United States in 1948,[[4]](#cite_note-sacks-4) the term had been used in *Billboard* as early as 1943.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) However, the company's first list of songs popular among African Americans was named [Harlem Hit Parade](/wiki/List_of_Harlem_Hit_Parade_number_ones_of_1942 "List of Harlem Hit Parade number ones of 1942"); created in 1942, it listed the "most popular records in [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem "Harlem")," and is the predecessor to the *Billboard* [RnB chart.](/wiki/R%26B_chart "R&B chart")[[7]](#cite_note-7) "Rhythm and Blues" replaced the common term "[race music](/wiki/Race_music "Race music")", a term coined by [Okeh](/wiki/Okeh_Records "Okeh Records") producer [Ralph Peer](/wiki/Ralph_Peer "Ralph Peer") based on the common self description by the African American press as "people of race."[[8]](#cite_note-Cohn-8)[[9]](#cite_note-wexler-9) The term "rhythm and blues" was then used by *Billboard* in [its chart listings](/wiki/Hot_R%26B/Hip-Hop_Songs "Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs") from June 1949 until August 1969, when its "Hot Rhythm & Blues Singles" chart was renamed as "Best Selling Soul Singles".[[10]](#cite_note-whitburnr&b-10) Before the term "rhythm and Blues" was popularized, various record companies had already begun replacing the term "race music" with the term "sepia series".[[11]](#cite_note-11) "Rhythm and blues" is often abbreviated as "R&B" or "R'n'B".[[12]](#cite_note-Randel1999-12) In subsequent decades, the abbreviation "R&B" evolved to encompass a broad range of popular music styles, including soul, funk, disco, and later contemporary R&B, reflecting shifts in production, audience, and industry marketing.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In the early 1950s, the term "rhythm & blues" was frequently applied to [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") records.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Writer and producer [Robert Palmer](/wiki/Robert_Palmer_(author/producer) "Robert Palmer (author/producer)") defined rhythm & blues as "a catchall term referring to any music that was made by and for black Americans".[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPalmer19958-15) He has also used the term "R&B" as a synonym for [jump blues](/wiki/Jump_blues "Jump blues").[[16]](#cite_note-deep_blues-16) However, [AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic") separates it from jump blues because of R&B's stronger gospel influences.[[17]](#cite_note-allmusic-17) [Lawrence Cohn](/wiki/Lawrence_Cohn "Lawrence Cohn"), author of *Nothing but the Blues*, writes that "rhythm and blues" was an umbrella term invented for industry convenience. According to him, the term embraced all black music except [classical music](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") and [religious music](/wiki/Religious_music "Religious music"), unless a gospel song sold enough to break into the charts.[[8]](#cite_note-Cohn-8) Well into the 21st century, the term R&B continues in use (in some contexts) to categorize music made by black musicians, as distinct from styles of music made by other musicians.

In the commercial rhythm and blues music typical of the 1950s through the 1970s, the bands usually consisted of piano, one or two guitars, bass, drums, and saxophone. Arrangements were rehearsed to the point of effortlessness and were sometimes accompanied by background vocalists. Simple repetitive parts mesh, creating momentum and rhythmic interplay producing mellow, lilting, and often hypnotic textures while calling attention to no individual sound. While singers are emotionally engaged with the lyrics, often intensely so, they remain cool, relaxed, and in control. The bands dressed in suits, and even uniforms, a practice associated with the modern popular music that rhythm and blues performers aspired to dominate. Lyrics often seemed fatalistic, and the music typically followed predictable patterns of chords and structure.[[18]](#cite_note-18) R&B lyrical themes often encapsulate the African-American experience of pain and the quest for freedom and joy,[[19]](#cite_note-19) as well as triumphs and failures in terms of relationships, economics, and aspirations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

One publication of the [Smithsonian Institution](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution "Smithsonian Institution") provided this summary of the origins of the genre in 2016.

> "A distinctly [African-American music](/wiki/African-American_music "African-American music") drawing from the deep tributaries of African American expressive culture, it is an amalgam of jump blues, big band swing, gospel, boogie, and blues that was initially developed during a thirty-year period that bridges the era of legally sanctioned racial segregation, international conflicts, and the struggle for civil rights".[[3]](#cite_note-folklife-3)

The Rock & Roll Hall of Fame defines some of the originators of R&B, including [Joe Turner](/wiki/Big_Joe_Turner "Big Joe Turner")'s big band, [Louis Jordan](/wiki/Louis_Jordan "Louis Jordan")'s [Tympany Five](/wiki/Tympany_Five "Tympany Five"), [James Brown](/wiki/James_Brown "James Brown") and [LaVern Baker](/wiki/LaVern_Baker "LaVern Baker"). In fact, this source states that "Louis Jordan joined Turner in laying the foundation for R&B in the 1940s, cutting one swinging rhythm & blues masterpiece after another". Other artists who were "cornerstones of R&B and its transformation into rock & roll" include [Etta James](/wiki/Etta_James "Etta James"), [Fats Domino](/wiki/Fats_Domino "Fats Domino"), [Roy Brown](/wiki/Roy_Brown_(blues_musician) "Roy Brown (blues musician)"), [Little Richard](/wiki/Little_Richard "Little Richard") and [Ruth Brown](/wiki/Ruth_Brown "Ruth Brown"). The "doo wop" groups were also noteworthy, including [the Orioles](/wiki/The_Orioles "The Orioles"), [the Ravens](/wiki/The_Ravens "The Ravens") and [the Dominoes](/wiki/Billy_Ward_and_his_Dominoes "Billy Ward and his Dominoes").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The term "rock and roll" had a strong sexual connotation in jump blues and R&B, but when DJ [Alan Freed](/wiki/Alan_Freed "Alan Freed") referred to rock and roll on mainstream radio in the mid-1950s, "the sexual component had been dialed down enough that it simply became an acceptable term for dancing".[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhythm_and_blues&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Precursors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhythm_and_blues&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Precursors")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Louis_Jordan%2C_New_York%2C_N.Y.%2C_ca._July_1946_%28William_P._Gottlieb_04721%29.jpg/250px-Louis_Jordan%2C_New_York%2C_N.Y.%2C_ca._July_1946_%28William_P._Gottlieb_04721%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louis_Jordan,_New_York,_N.Y.,_ca._July_1946_(William_P._Gottlieb_04721).jpg)

[Louis Jordan](/wiki/Louis_Jordan "Louis Jordan") in New York City, c. July 1946

The [great migration](/wiki/Great_migration_(African_American) "Great migration (African American)") of Black Americans to the urban industrial centers of Chicago, Detroit, New York City, Los Angeles, Washington, D.C. and elsewhere in the 1920s and 1930s created a new market for jazz, blues, and related genres of music. The migration of African Americans to urban centers in the 1920s and 1930s helped shape rhythm and blues by blending jazz, blues, and urban influences.[[22]](#cite_note-22) These genres of music were often performed by full-time musicians, either working alone or in small groups. The precursors of rhythm and blues came from jazz and blues, which overlapped in the late-1920s and 30s through the work of musicians such as the [Harlem Hamfats](/wiki/Harlem_Hamfats "Harlem Hamfats"), with their 1936 hit "Oh Red", as well as [Lonnie Johnson](/wiki/Lonnie_Johnson_(musician) "Lonnie Johnson (musician)"), [Leroy Carr](/wiki/Leroy_Carr "Leroy Carr"), [Cab Calloway](/wiki/Cab_Calloway "Cab Calloway"), [Count Basie](/wiki/Count_Basie "Count Basie"), and [T-Bone Walker](/wiki/T-Bone_Walker "T-Bone Walker"). There was also increasing emphasis on the [electric guitar](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar") as a lead instrument, as well as the [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano") and [saxophone](/wiki/Saxophone "Saxophone").[[23]](#cite_note-richards-23)

### Late 1940s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhythm_and_blues&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Late 1940s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Big_Joe_Turner_Rock_and_Roll_Revue_Apollo_Theater_1955.jpg/250px-Big_Joe_Turner_Rock_and_Roll_Revue_Apollo_Theater_1955.jpg)](/wiki/File:Big_Joe_Turner_Rock_and_Roll_Revue_Apollo_Theater_1955.jpg)

[Big Joe Turner](/wiki/Big_Joe_Turner "Big Joe Turner") was a key figure in early R&B, blending blues and rock.

R&B originated in [African-American](/wiki/African-American "African-American") communities in the 1940s.[[24]](#cite_note-24) In 1948, [RCA Victor](/wiki/RCA_Victor "RCA Victor") was marketing black music under the name "Blues and Rhythm". In that year, [Louis Jordan](/wiki/Louis_Jordan "Louis Jordan") dominated the top five listings of the [R&B charts](/wiki/Hot_R%26B/Hip-Hop_Songs "Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs") with three songs, and two of the top five songs were based on the [boogie-woogie](/wiki/Boogie-woogie_(music) "Boogie-woogie (music)") rhythms that had come to prominence during the 1940s.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Jordan's band, the [Tympany Five](/wiki/Tympany_Five "Tympany Five") (formed in 1938), consisted of him on saxophone and vocals, along with musicians on trumpet, tenor saxophone, piano, bass and drums.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) Lawrence Cohn described the music as "grittier than his boogie-era jazz-tinged blues".[[8]](#cite_note-Cohn-8): 173 Robert Palmer described it as "urbane, rocking, jazz-based music ... [with a] heavy, insistent beat".[[28]](#cite_note-palmer146-28) Jordan's music, along with that of [Big Joe Turner](/wiki/Big_Joe_Turner "Big Joe Turner"), [Roy Brown](/wiki/Roy_Brown_(blues_musician) "Roy Brown (blues musician)"), [Billy Wright](/wiki/Billy_Wright_(musician) "Billy Wright (musician)"), and [Wynonie Harris](/wiki/Wynonie_Harris "Wynonie Harris"), before 1949, was referred to as [jump blues](/wiki/Jump_blues "Jump blues"). Then, [Paul Gayten](/wiki/Paul_Gayten "Paul Gayten"), Roy Brown, and others had had hits in the style now referred to as rhythm and blues. In 1948, Wynonie Harris's remake of Brown's 1947 recording "[Good Rockin' Tonight](/wiki/Good_Rockin%27_Tonight "Good Rockin' Tonight")" reached number two on the charts, following [band leader](/wiki/Band_leader "Band leader") [Sonny Thompson](/wiki/Sonny_Thompson "Sonny Thompson")'s "Long Gone" at number one.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In 1949, the term "Rhythm and Blues" (R&B) replaced the Billboard category *Harlem Hit Parade*.[[8]](#cite_note-Cohn-8) Also in that year, "[The Huckle-Buck](/wiki/The_Huckle-Buck "The Huckle-Buck")", recorded by band leader and saxophonist [Paul Williams](/wiki/Paul_Williams_(saxophonist) "Paul Williams (saxophonist)"), was the number one R&B tune, remaining on top of the charts for nearly the entire year. Written by musician and arranger [Andy Gibson](/wiki/Andy_Gibson_(trumpeter) "Andy Gibson (trumpeter)"), the song was described as a "dirty boogie" because it was risque and raunchy.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Paul Williams and His Hucklebuckers' concerts were sweaty riotous affairs that got shut down on more than one occasion. Their lyrics, by [Roy Alfred](/wiki/Roy_Alfred "Roy Alfred") (who later co-wrote the 1955 hit "[(The) Rock and Roll Waltz](/wiki/(The)_Rock_and_Roll_Waltz "(The) Rock and Roll Waltz")"), were mildly sexually suggestive, and one teenager from Philadelphia said "That Hucklebuck was a very nasty dance".[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) Also in 1949, a new version of a 1920s blues song, "[Ain't Nobody's Business](/wiki/Ain%27t_Nobody%27s_Business "Ain't Nobody's Business")" was a number four hit for [Jimmy Witherspoon](/wiki/Jimmy_Witherspoon "Jimmy Witherspoon"), and Louis Jordan and the Tympany Five once again made the top five with "[Saturday Night Fish Fry](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Fish_Fry "Saturday Night Fish Fry")".[[34]](#cite_note-34) Many of these hit records were issued on new independent record labels, such as [Savoy](/wiki/Savoy_Records "Savoy Records") (founded 1942), [King](/wiki/King_Records_(USA) "King Records (USA)") (founded 1943), [Imperial](/wiki/Imperial_Records "Imperial Records") (founded 1945), [Specialty](/wiki/Specialty_Records "Specialty Records") (founded 1946), [Chess](/wiki/Chess_Records "Chess Records") (founded 1947), and [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Records "Atlantic Records") (founded 1948).[[23]](#cite_note-richards-23)

### Afro-Cuban rhythmic influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhythm_and_blues&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Afro-Cuban rhythmic influence")]

[African American music](/wiki/African_American_music "African American music") began incorporating Afro-Cuban rhythmic motifs in the 1800s with the popularity of the Cuban [contradanza](/wiki/Contradanza "Contradanza") (known outside of Cuba as the [habanera](/wiki/Habanera_(music) "Habanera (music)")).[[35]](#cite_note-35) The *habanera rhythm* can be thought of as a combination of [tresillo](/wiki/Tresillo_(rhythm) "Tresillo (rhythm)") and the [backbeat](/wiki/Backbeat "Backbeat").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Habanera_cut-time.jpg/330px-Habanera_cut-time.jpg)](/wiki/File:Habanera_cut-time.jpg)

The habanera rhythm shown as tresillo (lower notes) with the backbeat (upper note)

For the more than a quarter-century in which the [cakewalk](/wiki/Cakewalk "Cakewalk"), [ragtime](/wiki/Ragtime_music "Ragtime music") and proto-jazz were forming and developing, the Cuban genre *habanera* exerted a constant presence in African American popular music.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Jazz pioneer [Jelly Roll Morton](/wiki/Jelly_Roll_Morton "Jelly Roll Morton") considered the tresillo/habanera rhythm (which he called the [Spanish tinge](/wiki/Spanish_tinge "Spanish tinge")) to be an essential ingredient of jazz.[[37]](#cite_note-37) There are examples of tresillo-like rhythms in some African American folk music such as the hand-clapping and foot-stomping patterns in [ring shout](/wiki/Ring_shout "Ring shout"), post-Civil War drum and fife music, and [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") [second line](/wiki/Second_line_(parades) "Second line (parades)") music.[[38]](#cite_note-38) [Wynton Marsalis](/wiki/Wynton_Marsalis "Wynton Marsalis") considers tresillo to be the New Orleans "clave" (although technically, the pattern is only half a [clave](/wiki/Clave_(rhythm) "Clave (rhythm)")).[[39]](#cite_note-39) Tresillo is the most basic duple-pulse rhythmic [cell](/wiki/Cell_(music) "Cell (music)") in [Sub-Saharan African music traditions](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_African_music_traditions "Sub-Saharan African music traditions"), and its use in African American music is one of the clearest examples of African rhythmic retention in the United States.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The use of tresillo was continuously reinforced by the consecutive waves of Cuban music, which were adopted into North American popular culture. In 1940 Bob Zurke released "Rhumboogie", a boogie-woogie with a tresillo bass line, and lyrics proudly declaring the adoption of Cuban rhythm:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Dizzy_Gillespie01.JPG/250px-Dizzy_Gillespie01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Dizzy_Gillespie01.JPG)

[Dizzy Gillespie](/wiki/Dizzy_Gillespie "Dizzy Gillespie") was instrumental in blending Afro-Cuban rhythms with jazz.

> Harlem's got a new rhythm, man it's burning up the dance floors because it's so hot! They took a little rhumba rhythm and added boogie-woogie and now look what they got! Rhumboogie, it's Harlem's new creation with the Cuban syncopation, it's the killer! Just plant your both feet on each side. Let both your hips and shoulder glide. Then throw your body back and ride. There's nothing like rhumbaoogie, rhumboogie, boogie-woogie. In Harlem or Havana, you can kiss the old Savannah. It's a killer![[41]](#cite_note-41)

Although originating in the metropolis at the mouth of the Mississippi River, New Orleans blues, with its Afro-Caribbean rhythmic traits, is distinct from the sound of the Mississippi Delta blues.[[42]](#cite_note-42) In the late 1940s, New Orleans musicians were especially receptive to Cuban influences precisely at the time when R&B was first forming.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The first use of tresillo in R&B occurred in New Orleans. [Robert Palmer](/wiki/Robert_Palmer "Robert Palmer"), who sampled R&B on his album [*Don't Explain*](/wiki/Don%27t_Explain_(Robert_Palmer_album) "Don't Explain (Robert Palmer album)"), recalls:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Fats_Domino_1956.png/250px-Fats_Domino_1956.png)](/wiki/File:Fats_Domino_1956.png)

[Fats Domino](/wiki/Fats_Domino "Fats Domino") in 1956

> New Orleans producer-bandleader [Dave Bartholomew](/wiki/Dave_Bartholomew "Dave Bartholomew") first employed this figure (as a saxophone-section riff) on his own 1949 disc "Country Boy" and subsequently helped make it the most over-used rhythmic pattern in 1950s rock 'n' roll. On numerous recordings by [Fats Domino](/wiki/Fats_Domino "Fats Domino"), [Little Richard](/wiki/Little_Richard "Little Richard") and others, Bartholomew assigned this repeating three-note pattern not just to the string bass, but also to electric guitars and even baritone sax, making for a very heavy bottom. He recalls first hearing the figure – as a bass pattern on a Cuban disc.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPalmer199560-44)

In a 1988 interview, Bartholomew (who had the first R&B studio band),[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESublette200782-45) revealed how he initially superimposed tresillo over swing rhythm:

> I heard the bass playing that part on a 'rumba' record. On 'Country Boy' I had my bass and drums playing a straight swing rhythm and wrote out that 'rumba' bass part for the saxes to play on top of the swing rhythm. Later, especially after rock 'n' roll came along, I made the 'rumba' bass part heavier and heavier. I'd have the string bass, an electric guitar and a baritone all in unison.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Bartholomew referred to the Cuban [son](/wiki/Son_music "Son music") by the misnomer *rumba*, a common practice of that time. Fats Domino's "[Blue Monday](/wiki/Blue_Monday_(1954_song) "Blue Monday (1954 song)")", produced by Bartholomew, is another example of this now classic use of tresillo in R&B. Bartholomew's 1949 tresillo-based "Oh Cubanas" is an attempt to blend African American and Afro-Cuban music. The word *mambo*, larger than any of the other text, is placed prominently on the record label. In his composition "Misery", New Orleans pianist [Professor Longhair](/wiki/Professor_Longhair "Professor Longhair") plays a habanera-like figure in his left hand.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The deft use of triplets is a characteristic of Longhair's style.

[Gerhard Kubik](/wiki/Gerhard_Kubik "Gerhard Kubik") notes that with the exception of New Orleans, early blues lacked complex polyrhythms, and there was a "very specific absence of asymmetric time-line patterns ([key patterns](/wiki/Bell_pattern "Bell pattern")) in virtually all early-twentieth-century [African-American music](/wiki/African-American_music "African-American music") ... only in some New Orleans genres does a hint of simple time line patterns occasionally appear in the form of transient so-called 'stomp' patterns or stop-time chorus. These do not function in the same way as African timelines."[[48]](#cite_note-48) In the late 1940s, this changed somewhat when the two-celled timeline structure was brought into the blues. New Orleans musicians such as Bartholomew and Longhair incorporated Cuban instruments, as well as the clave pattern and related two-celled figures in songs such as "Carnival Day", (Bartholomew 1949) and "Mardi Gras In New Orleans" (Longhair 1949). While some of these early experiments were awkward fusions, the Afro-Cuban elements were eventually integrated fully into the New Orleans sound.

In the 1940s, Professor Longhair listened to and played with musicians from the islands and "fell under the spell of Perez Prado's [mambo](/wiki/Mambo_(music) "Mambo (music)") records."[[49]](#cite_note-49) He was especially enamored with Afro-Cuban music. Michael Campbell states: "Professor Longhair's influence was ... far-reaching. In several of his early recordings, Professor Longhair blended Afro-Cuban rhythms with rhythm and blues. The most explicit is 'Longhair's Blues Rhumba,' where he overlays a straightforward blues with a clave rhythm."[[50]](#cite_note-50) Longhair's particular style was known locally as *rumba-boogie*.[[51]](#cite_note-51) In his "Mardi Gras in New Orleans", the pianist employs the 2–3 clave onbeat/offbeat motif in a rumba boogie "[guajeo](/wiki/Guajeo "Guajeo")".[[52]](#cite_note-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Mardi_gras_in_new_orleans.tif/lossy-page1-500px-Mardi_gras_in_new_orleans.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mardi_gras_in_new_orleans.tif)

Piano excerpt from the rumba boogie "Mardi Gras in New Orleans" (1949) by Professor Longhair. 2–3 claves are written above for rhythmic reference.

The syncopated, but straight subdivision feel of Cuban music (as opposed to swung subdivisions) took root in New Orleans R&B during this time. Alexander Stewart states that the popular feel was passed along from "New Orleans—through James Brown's music, to the popular music of the 1970s," adding: "The singular style of rhythm & blues that emerged from New Orleans in the years after World War II played an important role in the development of funk. In a related development, the underlying rhythms of American popular music underwent a basic, yet generally unacknowledged transition from a triplet or shuffle feel to even or straight eighth notes.[[53]](#cite_note-53) Concerning the various funk motifs, Stewart states that this model "is different from a [time line](/wiki/Bell_pattern "Bell pattern") (such as clave and tresillo) in that it is not an exact pattern, but more of a loose organizing principle."[[54]](#cite_note-54)

[Johnny Otis](/wiki/Johnny_Otis "Johnny Otis") released the R&B mambo "Mambo Boogie" in January 1951, featuring congas, maracas, claves, and mambo saxophone [guajeos](/wiki/Guajeo "Guajeo") in a blues progression.[[55]](#cite_note-55) [Ike Turner](/wiki/Ike_Turner "Ike Turner") recorded "Cubano Jump" (1954) an electric guitar instrumental, which is built around several 2–3 clave figures, adopted from the mambo. [The Hawketts](/wiki/The_Hawketts "The Hawketts"), in "[Mardi Gras Mambo](/wiki/Mardi_Gras_Mambo "Mardi Gras Mambo")" (1955) (featuring the vocals of a young Art Neville), make a clear reference to Perez Prado in their use of his trademark "Unhh!" in the break after the introduction.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

[Ned Sublette](/wiki/Ned_Sublette "Ned Sublette") states: "The electric blues cats were very well aware of Latin music, and there was definitely such a thing as *rhumba blues*; you can hear Muddy Waters and Howlin' Wolf playing it."[[57]](#cite_note-Sublette,_Ned_2007_p._83-57) He also cites [Otis Rush](/wiki/Otis_Rush "Otis Rush"), [Ike Turner](/wiki/Ike_Turner "Ike Turner") and [Ray Charles](/wiki/Ray_Charles "Ray Charles"), as R&B artists who employed this feel.[[57]](#cite_note-Sublette,_Ned_2007_p._83-57)

The use of clave in R&B coincided with the growing dominance of the [backbeat](/wiki/Back_beat_(music) "Back beat (music)"), and the rising popularity of Cuban music in the U.S. In a sense, clave can be distilled down to tresillo (three-side) answered by the backbeat (two-side).[[58]](#cite_note-58)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Clave_in_cut-time.tiff/lossy-page1-500px-Clave_in_cut-time.tiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clave_in_cut-time.tiff)

3–2 clave written in two measures in cut-time

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Tresillo_and_backbeat.tiff/lossy-page1-500px-Tresillo_and_backbeat.tiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tresillo_and_backbeat.tiff)

Tresillo answered by the backbeat, the essence of clave in African American music

The "[Bo Diddley beat](/wiki/Bo_Diddley_beat "Bo Diddley beat")" (1955) is perhaps the first true fusion of [3–2 clave](/wiki/Clave_(rhythm) "Clave (rhythm)") and R&B/rock 'n' roll. [Bo Diddley](/wiki/Bo_Diddley "Bo Diddley") has given different accounts of the riff's origins. Sublette asserts: "In the context of the time, and especially those maracas [heard on the record], 'Bo Diddley' has to be understood as a Latin-tinged record. A rejected cut recorded at the same session was titled only 'Rhumba' on the track sheets."[[57]](#cite_note-Sublette,_Ned_2007_p._83-57) [Johnny Otis](/wiki/Johnny_Otis "Johnny Otis")'s "Willie and the Hand Jive" (1958) is another example of this successful blend of 3–2 claves and R&B. Otis used the Cuban instruments claves and maracas on the song.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Bo-Diddley.jpg/250px-Bo-Diddley.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bo-Diddley.jpg)

[Bo Diddley](/wiki/Bo_Diddley "Bo Diddley")'s "[Bo Diddley beat](/wiki/Bo_Diddley_beat "Bo Diddley beat")" is a clave-based motif.

Afro-Cuban music was the conduit by which African American music was "re-Africanized", through the adoption of two-celled figures like clave and Afro-Cuban instruments like the [conga drum](/wiki/Conga_drum "Conga drum"), [bongos](/wiki/Bongos "Bongos"), [maracas](/wiki/Maracas "Maracas") and [claves](/wiki/Claves "Claves"). According to [John Storm Roberts](/wiki/John_Storm_Roberts "John Storm Roberts"), R&B became the vehicle for the return of Cuban elements into mass popular music.[[59]](#cite_note-59) [Ahmet Ertegun](/wiki/Ahmet_Ertegun "Ahmet Ertegun"), producer for [Atlantic Records](/wiki/Atlantic_Records "Atlantic Records"), is reported to have said that "Afro-Cuban rhythms added color and excitement to the basic drive of R&B."[[60]](#cite_note-60) As [Ned Sublette](/wiki/Ned_Sublette "Ned Sublette") points out though: "By the 1960s, with Cuba the object of a United States embargo that still remains in effect today, the island nation had been forgotten as a source of music. By the time people began to talk about rock and roll as having a history, Cuban music had vanished from North American consciousness."[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESublette200769-61)

### Early to mid-1950s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhythm_and_blues&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early to mid-1950s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Little_Richard_%281967%29.png/250px-Little_Richard_%281967%29.png)](/wiki/File:Little_Richard_(1967).png)

[Little Richard](/wiki/Little_Richard "Little Richard") in 1967

At first, only African Americans were buying R&B discs. According to [Jerry Wexler](/wiki/Jerry_Wexler "Jerry Wexler") of Atlantic Records, sales were localized in African-American markets; there were no white sales or white radio play. During the early 1950s, more white teenagers started to become aware of R&B and began purchasing the music. For example, 40% of 1952 sales at [Dolphin's of Hollywood](/wiki/John_Dolphin_(music_producer) "John Dolphin (music producer)") record shop, located in an African-American area of Los Angeles, were to whites. Eventually, white teens across the country turned their musical taste toward rhythm and blues.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

[Johnny Otis](/wiki/Johnny_Otis "Johnny Otis"), who had signed with the Newark, New Jersey–based Savoy Records, produced many R&B hits in 1951, including "[Double Crossing Blues](/wiki/Double_Crossing_Blues "Double Crossing Blues")", "Mistrustin' Blues" and "[Cupid's Boogie](/wiki/Cupid%27s_Boogie "Cupid's Boogie")", all of which hit number one that year. Otis scored ten top ten hits that year. Other hits include "[Gee Baby](/wiki/Gee_Baby "Gee Baby")", "Mambo Boogie" and "All Nite Long".[[63]](#cite_note-billboard.com-63) [The Clovers](/wiki/The_Clovers "The Clovers"), a quintet consisting of a vocal quartet with accompanying guitarist, sang a distinctive-sounding combination of blues and gospel.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_3,_track_2-64) They had the number five hit of the year with "[Don't You Know I Love You](/wiki/Don%27t_You_Know_I_Love_You "Don't You Know I Love You")" on Atlantic.[[63]](#cite_note-billboard.com-63)[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66) Also in July 1951, Cleveland, Ohio DJ [Alan Freed](/wiki/Alan_Freed "Alan Freed") started a late-night radio show called "The Moondog Rock Roll House Party" on [WJW](/wiki/WKNR "WKNR") (850 AM).[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68) Freed's show was sponsored by Fred Mintz, whose R&B record store had a primarily African-American clientele. Freed began referring to the rhythm and blues music he played as "rock and roll".

In 1951 [Little Richard](/wiki/Little_Richard "Little Richard") Penniman began recording for [RCA Records](/wiki/RCA_Records "RCA Records") in the jump blues style of late 1940s stars [Roy Brown](/wiki/Roy_Brown_(blues_musician) "Roy Brown (blues musician)") and [Billy Wright](/wiki/Billy_Wright_(musician) "Billy Wright (musician)"). However, it was not until he recorded a demo in 1954 that caught the attention of Specialty Records that the world would start to hear his new uptempo funky rhythm and blues that would catapult him to fame in 1955 and help define the sound of rock 'n' roll. A rapid succession of rhythm and blues hits followed, beginning with "[Tutti Frutti](/wiki/Tutti_Frutti_(song) "Tutti Frutti (song)")"[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_6,_track_2-69) and "[Long Tall Sally](/wiki/Long_Tall_Sally "Long Tall Sally")", which would influence performers such as [James Brown](/wiki/James_Brown "James Brown"),[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2003231-70) [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley"),[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2003227-71) and [Otis Redding](/wiki/Otis_Redding "Otis Redding").[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2003231-70)

Also in 1951, the song [Rocket 88](/wiki/Rocket_88 "Rocket 88") was recorded by [Ike Turner](/wiki/Ike_Turner "Ike Turner") and his Kings of Rhythm at a studio owned by [Sam Phillips](/wiki/Sam_Phillips "Sam Phillips") with the vocal by [Jackie Brenston](/wiki/Jackie_Brenston "Jackie Brenston"). This song is often cited as a precursor to [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll") or as one of the first records in that genre.[[72]](#cite_note-72) In a later interview, however, Ike Turner offered this comment: "I don't think that 'Rocket 88' is rock 'n' roll. I think that 'Rocket 88' is R&B, but I think 'Rocket 88' is the *cause* of rock and roll existing".[[73]](#cite_note-73)

[Ruth Brown](/wiki/Ruth_Brown "Ruth Brown"), performing on the Atlantic label, placed hits in the top five every year from 1951 through 1954: "[Teardrops from My Eyes](/wiki/Teardrops_from_My_Eyes "Teardrops from My Eyes")", "Five, Ten, Fifteen Hours", "[(Mama) He Treats Your Daughter Mean](/wiki/(Mama)_He_Treats_Your_Daughter_Mean "(Mama) He Treats Your Daughter Mean")" and "[What a Dream](/wiki/What_a_Dream "What a Dream")".[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_3,_track_2-64) [Faye Adams](/wiki/Faye_Adams "Faye Adams")'s "[Shake a Hand](/wiki/Shake_a_Hand "Shake a Hand")" made it to number two in 1952. In 1953, the R&B record-buying public made [Big Mama Thornton](/wiki/Big_Mama_Thornton "Big Mama Thornton")'s original recording of [Leiber and Stoller](/wiki/Leiber_and_Stoller "Leiber and Stoller")'s "[Hound Dog](/wiki/Hound_Dog_(song) "Hound Dog (song)")"[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_7,_track_4-74) the year's number three hit. Ruth Brown was very prominent among female R&B stars; her popularity most likely came from "her deeply rooted vocal delivery in African American tradition".[[75]](#cite_note-Floyd177-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76) That same year [The Orioles](/wiki/The_Orioles "The Orioles"), a [doo-wop](/wiki/Doo-wop "Doo-wop") group, had the number four hit of the year with "[Crying in the Chapel](/wiki/Crying_in_the_Chapel "Crying in the Chapel")".[[77]](#cite_note-77)

[Fats Domino](/wiki/Fats_Domino "Fats Domino") made the top 30 of the pop charts in 1952 and 1953, then the top 10 with "[Ain't That a Shame](/wiki/Ain%27t_That_a_Shame "Ain't That a Shame")".[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_6,_track_3-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79) [Ray Charles](/wiki/Ray_Charles "Ray Charles") came to national prominence in 1955 with "[I Got a Woman](/wiki/I_Got_a_Woman "I Got a Woman")".[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_15-80) [Big Bill Broonzy](/wiki/Big_Bill_Broonzy "Big Bill Broonzy") said of Charles's music: "He's mixing the blues with the spirituals ... I know that's wrong."[[8]](#cite_note-Cohn-8): 173

In 1954 [the Chords](/wiki/The_Chords_(US_band) "The Chords (US band)")' "[Sh-Boom](/wiki/Sh-Boom "Sh-Boom")"[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_4,_track_5-81) became the first hit to cross over from the R&B chart to hit the top 10 early in the year. Late in the year, and into 1955, "[Hearts of Stone](/wiki/Hearts_of_Stone "Hearts of Stone")" by the Charms made the top 20.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

At [Chess Records](/wiki/Chess_Records "Chess Records") in the spring of 1955, [Bo Diddley](/wiki/Bo_Diddley "Bo Diddley")'s debut record "[Bo Diddley](/wiki/Bo_Diddley_(Bo_Diddley_song) "Bo Diddley (Bo Diddley song)")"/"[I'm a Man](/wiki/I%27m_a_Man_(Bo_Diddley_song) "I'm a Man (Bo Diddley song)")" climbed to number two on the R&B charts and popularized Bo Diddley's own original rhythm and blues clave-based vamp that would become a mainstay in rock and roll.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_3,_track_5-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Etta_James04.JPG/250px-Etta_James04.JPG)](/wiki/File:Etta_James04.JPG)

[Etta James](/wiki/Etta_James "Etta James") blended blues, R&B, and gospel influences.

At the urging of [Leonard Chess](/wiki/Leonard_Chess "Leonard Chess") at Chess Records, [Chuck Berry](/wiki/Chuck_Berry "Chuck Berry") reworked a [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") fiddle tune with a long history, entitled "[Ida Red](/wiki/Ida_Red_(song) "Ida Red (song)")".[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_5,_track_5-84) The resulting "[Maybellene](/wiki/Maybellene "Maybellene")" was not only a number three hit on the R&B charts in 1955, but also reached into the top 30 on the pop charts. [Alan Freed](/wiki/Alan_Freed "Alan Freed"), who had moved to the much larger market of New York City in 1954, helped the record become popular with white teenagers. Freed had been given part of the writing credit by Chess in return for his promotional activities, a common practice at the time.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

R&B was also a strong influence on [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll").[[86]](#cite_note-86) A 1985 article in *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")*, titled, "Rock! It's Still Rhythm and Blues"[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] reported that the "two terms were used interchangeably" until about 1957. The other sources quoted in the article said that rock and roll combined R&B with pop and country music.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

Fats Domino was not convinced that there was any new genre. In 1957, he said, "What they call rock 'n' roll now is rhythm and blues. I've been playing it for 15 years in New Orleans".[[88]](#cite_note-88) According to *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone_(magazine) "Rolling Stone (magazine)")*, "this is a valid statement ... all Fifties rockers, black and white, country born and city bred, were fundamentally influenced by R&B, the black popular music of the late Forties and early Fifties".[[89]](#cite_note-89)

### Late 1950s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhythm_and_blues&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Late 1950s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Della_Reese_Jubilee_Records.jpg/250px-Della_Reese_Jubilee_Records.jpg)](/wiki/File:Della_Reese_Jubilee_Records.jpg)

[Della Reese](/wiki/Della_Reese "Della Reese")

In 1956, an R&B "Top Stars of '56" tour took place, with headliners [Al Hibbler](/wiki/Al_Hibbler "Al Hibbler"), [Frankie Lymon](/wiki/Frankie_Lymon "Frankie Lymon") and the Teenagers, and [Carl Perkins](/wiki/Carl_Perkins "Carl Perkins"), whose "[Blue Suede Shoes](/wiki/Blue_Suede_Shoes "Blue Suede Shoes")" was very popular with R&B music buyers.[[90]](#cite_note-90) Some of the performers completing the bill were Chuck Berry, [Cathy Carr](/wiki/Cathy_Carr_(singer) "Cathy Carr (singer)"), [Shirley & Lee](/wiki/Shirley_%26_Lee "Shirley & Lee"), [Della Reese](/wiki/Della_Reese "Della Reese"), Sam "T-Bird" Jensen, [the Cleftones](/wiki/The_Cleftones "The Cleftones"), and [the Spaniels](/wiki/The_Spaniels "The Spaniels") with [Illinois Jacquet](/wiki/Illinois_Jacquet "Illinois Jacquet")'s Big Rockin' Rhythm Band.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Cities visited by the tour included Columbia, South Carolina; Annapolis, Maryland; Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania; Syracuse, Rochester and Buffalo, New York; and other cities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In Columbia, the concert ended with a near riot as Perkins began his first song as the closing act. Perkins is quoted as saying, "It was dangerous. Lot of kids got hurt". In Annapolis, 50,000 to 70,000 people tried to attend a sold-out performance with 8,000 seats. Roads were clogged for seven hours.[[92]](#cite_note-92)
Filmmakers took advantage of the popularity of "rhythm and blues" musicians as "rock n roll" musicians beginning in 1956. Little Richard, Chuck Berry, Fats Domino, Big Joe Turner, [the Treniers](/wiki/The_Treniers "The Treniers"), [the Platters](/wiki/The_Platters "The Platters"), and [the Flamingos](/wiki/The_Flamingos "The Flamingos") all made it onto the big screen.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

Two Elvis Presley records made the R&B top five in 1957: "[Jailhouse Rock](/wiki/Jailhouse_Rock_(song) "Jailhouse Rock (song)")"/"[Treat Me Nice](/wiki/Treat_Me_Nice "Treat Me Nice")" at number one, and "[All Shook Up](/wiki/All_Shook_Up "All Shook Up")" at number five, an unprecedented acceptance of a non-African American artist into a music category known for being created by blacks.[[94]](#cite_note-94) [Nat King Cole](/wiki/Nat_King_Cole "Nat King Cole"), also a [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") pianist who had two hits on the pop charts in the early 1950s ("[Mona Lisa](/wiki/Mona_Lisa_(Nat_King_Cole_song) "Mona Lisa (Nat King Cole song)")" at number two in 1950 and "[Too Young](/wiki/Too_Young_(1951_song) "Too Young (1951 song)")" at number one in 1951), had a record in the top five in the R&B charts in 1958, "[Looking Back](/wiki/Looking_Back_(Nat_King_Cole_song) "Looking Back (Nat King Cole song)")"/"Do I Like It".[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_22,_tracks_3–4-95)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Sam_Cooke_2.jpg/250px-Sam_Cooke_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sam_Cooke_2.jpg)

[Sam Cooke](/wiki/Sam_Cooke "Sam Cooke")

In 1959, two black-owned record labels, one of which would become hugely successful, made their debut: [Sam Cooke](/wiki/Sam_Cooke "Sam Cooke")'s Sar and [Berry Gordy](/wiki/Berry_Gordy "Berry Gordy")'s [Motown Records](/wiki/Motown_Records "Motown Records").[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPalmer199582-96) [Brook Benton](/wiki/Brook_Benton "Brook Benton") was at the top of the R&B charts in 1959 and 1960 with one number one and two number two hits.[[97]](#cite_note-97) Benton had a certain warmth in his voice that attracted a wide variety of listeners, and his ballads led to comparisons with performers such as
[Nat King Cole](/wiki/Nat_King_Cole "Nat King Cole"), [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra") and [Tony Bennett](/wiki/Tony_Bennett "Tony Bennett").[[98]](#cite_note-98) [Lloyd Price](/wiki/Lloyd_Price "Lloyd Price"), who in 1952 had a number one hit with "[Lawdy Miss Clawdy](/wiki/Lawdy_Miss_Clawdy "Lawdy Miss Clawdy")", regained predominance with a version of "[Stagger Lee](/wiki/Stagger_Lee_(song) "Stagger Lee (song)")" at number one and "[Personality](/wiki/Personality_(Lloyd_Price_song) "Personality (Lloyd Price song)")" at number five in 1959.[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-Hot_songs_1959-100)

The white bandleader of the Bill Black Combo, [Bill Black](/wiki/Bill_Black "Bill Black"), who had helped start Elvis Presley's career and was Elvis's bassist in the 1950s, had a hit among black listeners with "[Smokie, Part 2](/wiki/Smokie,_Part_2 "Smokie, Part 2")". The single reached number 17 on the U.S. pop charts and number 1 on the R&B charts, selling over one million copies, and was awarded a [gold disc](/wiki/Music_recording_sales_certification "Music recording sales certification") by the [RIAA](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America").[[101]](#cite_note-101) [Hi Records](/wiki/Hi_Records "Hi Records") did not feature pictures of the Combo on early records.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

### 1960s–1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhythm_and_blues&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1960s–1970s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Marvin_Gaye_%281973%29.png/250px-Marvin_Gaye_%281973%29.png)](/wiki/File:Marvin_Gaye_(1973).png)

[Marvin Gaye](/wiki/Marvin_Gaye "Marvin Gaye") is a R&B artist known for his smooth voice.

[Sam Cooke](/wiki/Sam_Cooke "Sam Cooke")'s number five hit "[Chain Gang](/wiki/Chain_Gang_(Sam_Cooke_song) "Chain Gang (Sam Cooke song)")" is indicative of R&B in 1960, as is pop rocker [Chubby Checker](/wiki/Chubby_Checker "Chubby Checker")'s number five hit "[The Twist](/wiki/The_Twist_(song) "The Twist (song)")".[[100]](#cite_note-Hot_songs_1959-100)[[103]](#cite_note-103) By the early 1960s, the music industry category previously known as rhythm and blues was being called [soul music](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"), and similar music by white artists was labeled [blue-eyed soul](/wiki/Blue-eyed_soul "Blue-eyed soul").[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_52-104)[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPalmer199582-96) Motown Records had its first million-selling single in 1960 with [the Miracles](/wiki/The_Miracles "The Miracles")' "[Shop Around](/wiki/Shop_Around "Shop Around")",[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_25-105) and in 1961, [Stax Records](/wiki/Stax_Records "Stax Records") had its first hit with [Carla Thomas](/wiki/Carla_Thomas "Carla Thomas")'s "[Gee Whiz (Look at His Eyes)](/wiki/Gee_Whiz_(Look_at_His_Eyes) "Gee Whiz (Look at His Eyes)")".[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPalmer199583–84-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107) Stax's next major hit, [the Mar-Keys](/wiki/The_Mar-Keys "The Mar-Keys")' instrumental "[Last Night](/wiki/Last_Night_(Mar-Keys_composition) "Last Night (Mar-Keys composition)")" (also released in 1961), introduced the rawer [Memphis soul](/wiki/Memphis_soul "Memphis soul") sound for which Stax became known.[[108]](#cite_note-108) In Jamaica, R&B influenced the development of [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska").[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110) In 1969, black culture and rhythm and blues reached another great achievement when [the Grammys](/wiki/The_Grammys "The Grammys") added the Rhythm and Blues category, giving academic recognition to the category.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

By the 1970s, the term "rhythm and blues" was being used as a blanket term for [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"), [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"), and [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco").[[111]](#cite_note-111)

### Since the 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhythm_and_blues&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Since the 1980s")]

Main article: [Contemporary R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Mary_J._Blige_jul2007.jpg/250px-Mary_J._Blige_jul2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mary_J._Blige_jul2007.jpg)

[Mary J. Blige](/wiki/Mary_J._Blige "Mary J. Blige") blended R&B with hip-hop influences, helping to shape the sound of the '90s.

In the late 1980s and early 1990s, [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") started to capture the imagination of America's youth. R&B started to become homogenized, with a group of high-profile producers responsible for most R&B hits. It was hard for R&B artists of the era to sell their music or even have their music heard because of the rise of hip-hop, but some adopted a "hip-hop" image, were marketed as such, and often featured rappers on their songs. [Teddy Riley](/wiki/Teddy_Riley "Teddy Riley"), Guy, [Keith Sweat](/wiki/Keith_Sweat "Keith Sweat") and Today gained [new jack swing](/wiki/New_jack_swing "New jack swing") hits.[[112]](#cite_note-112) In 1990, *Billboard* reintroduced R&B to categorize all of Black popular music other than hip-hop.[[113]](#cite_note-113) Newer artists such as [Usher](/wiki/Usher_(singer) "Usher (singer)"), [R. Kelly](/wiki/R._Kelly "R. Kelly"), [Janet Jackson](/wiki/Janet_Jackson "Janet Jackson"), [TLC](/wiki/TLC_(group) "TLC (group)"), [Aaliyah](/wiki/Aaliyah "Aaliyah"), [Brandy](/wiki/Brandy_Norwood "Brandy Norwood"), [Destiny's Child](/wiki/Destiny%27s_Child "Destiny's Child"), [Tevin Campbell](/wiki/Tevin_Campbell "Tevin Campbell") and [Mary J. Blige](/wiki/Mary_J._Blige "Mary J. Blige") enjoyed success. [L.A. Reid](/wiki/L.A._Reid "L.A. Reid"), the CEO of [LaFace Records](/wiki/LaFace_Records "LaFace Records"), was responsible for some of R&B's greatest successes in the 1990s in the form of [Usher](/wiki/Usher_(musician) "Usher (musician)"), [TLC](/wiki/TLC_(group) "TLC (group)") and [Toni Braxton](/wiki/Toni_Braxton "Toni Braxton"). Later, Reid successfully marketed [Boyz II Men](/wiki/Boyz_II_Men "Boyz II Men").[[114]](#cite_note-THE_CHANGING_FACE_OF_R&B-114) In 2004, 80% of the songs that topped the R&B charts were also at the top of the Hot 100. That period was the all-time peak for R&B and hip hop on the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") and on Top 40 Radio.[[115]](#cite_note-115) From about 2005 to 2013, R&B sales declined.[[116]](#cite_note-116) However, since 2010, hip-hop has started to take cues from the R&B sound, choosing to adopt a softer, smoother sound that incorporates traditional R&B with rappers such as [Drake](/wiki/Drake_(musician) "Drake (musician)"), who has opened an entire new door for the genre. This sound has gained in popularity and created great controversy for both hip-hop and R&B as to how to identify it.[[117]](#cite_note-117) In 2010, the [National Rhythm & Blues Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Rhythm_%26_Blues_Hall_of_Fame "National Rhythm & Blues Hall of Fame") was founded by [LaMont "ShowBoat" Robinson](/wiki/LaMont_%22ShowBoat%22_Robinson "LaMont \"ShowBoat\" Robinson").[[118]](#cite_note-118)

## Jewish influence in the business end of rhythm and blues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhythm_and_blues&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Jewish influence in the business end of rhythm and blues")]

Main article: [Jewish influence in rhythm and blues](/wiki/Jewish_influence_in_rhythm_and_blues "Jewish influence in rhythm and blues")

According to the Jewish writer, music publishing executive, and songwriter [Arnold Shaw](/wiki/Arnold_Shaw_(writer) "Arnold Shaw (writer)"), during the 1940s in the US, there was generally little opportunity for Jews in the [WASP](/wiki/WASP "WASP")-controlled realm of [mass communications](/wiki/Mass_media "Mass media"), but the music business was "wide open for Jews as it was for blacks".[[119]](#cite_note-Shaw1978-119) Jews played a key role in developing and popularizing African American music, including rhythm and blues, and the independent record business was dominated by young Jewish men who promoted the sounds of black music.[[120]](#cite_note-Muchin1994-120)

For example, Milt Gabler was born to Jewish immigrants and grew up in Harlem. In the late 1930s, he created his own record label, [Commodore Records](/wiki/Commodore_Records "Commodore Records"), primarily recording, producing and promoting black singers and groups. Gabler recorded many of the leading jazz artists of the day.[[121]](#cite_note-121) In 1939, he released the [Billie Holiday](/wiki/Billie_Holiday "Billie Holiday") hit song "[Strange Fruit](/wiki/Strange_Fruit "Strange Fruit")" on Commodore, boosting her career with what [*Time*](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)") magazine 60 years later named 'Best Song of the Century’. Based on the success of Commodore, the major label [Decca Records](/wiki/Decca_Records "Decca Records") recruited and hired Gabler in 1941.[[122]](#cite_note-122) His Jewish brother-in-law, Jack Crystal, (father of [Billy Crystal](/wiki/Billy_Crystal "Billy Crystal")), took over Commodore.

At Decca, Gabler worked with many of the biggest stars of the 1940s, producing a series of hits, as well as being the first to bring [Louis Armstrong](/wiki/Louis_Armstrong "Louis Armstrong") and [Ella Fitzgerald](/wiki/Ella_Fitzgerald "Ella Fitzgerald") together on record. Decca then placed him in charge of their subsidiary label [Coral](/wiki/Coral_Records "Coral Records"), where he continued to advance the careers of black musicians including [the Ink Spots](/wiki/The_Ink_Spots "The Ink Spots"), [Sammy Davis Jr.](/wiki/Sammy_Davis_Jr. "Sammy Davis Jr.") and [Louis Jordan](/wiki/Louis_Jordan "Louis Jordan").

Gabler was responsible for many innovations in the recording industry of the 20th century. These included being the first person to deal in record reissues, the first to sell records by mail order, and the first to credit all the musicians on the recordings. In 1991, Gabler received the [Grammy Trustees Award](/wiki/Grammy_Trustees_Award "Grammy Trustees Award") from [The Recording Academy](/wiki/The_Recording_Academy "The Recording Academy"), for his significant contributions to the field of recording.[[123]](#cite_note-123) In 1993, he was inducted into the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame") by his nephew Billy Crystal.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

## British rhythm and blues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhythm_and_blues&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: British rhythm and blues")]

Main article: [British rhythm and blues](/wiki/British_rhythm_and_blues "British rhythm and blues")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Eric_Burdon_%26_the_Animals.jpg/250px-Eric_Burdon_%26_the_Animals.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eric_Burdon_%26_the_Animals.jpg)

[Eric Burdon](/wiki/Eric_Burdon "Eric Burdon") & [the Animals](/wiki/The_Animals "The Animals") (1964)

British rhythm and blues and blues rock developed in the early 1960s, largely as a response to the recordings of American artists, often brought over by African American servicemen stationed in Britain or seamen visiting ports such as London, Liverpool, Newcastle and Belfast.[[125]](#cite_note-125)[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_27-126) Many bands, particularly in the developing London club scene, tried to emulate black rhythm and blues performers, resulting in a "rawer" or "grittier" sound than the more popular "[beat groups](/wiki/Beat_music "Beat music")".[[127]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002BritishR&B-127) During the 1960s, [Geno Washington](/wiki/Geno_Washington "Geno Washington"), [the Foundations](/wiki/The_Foundations "The Foundations"), and [the Equals](/wiki/The_Equals "The Equals") gained pop hits.[[128]](#cite_note-128) Many British black musicians helped form the British R&B scene. These included [Geno Washington](/wiki/Geno_Washington "Geno Washington"), an American singer stationed in England with the Air Force. He was invited to join what became [Geno Washington & the Ram Jam Band](/wiki/Geno_Washington_%26_the_Ram_Jam_Band "Geno Washington & the Ram Jam Band") by guitarist [Pete Gage](/wiki/Pete_Gage_(guitarist) "Pete Gage (guitarist)") in 1965 and enjoyed top 40 hit singles and two top 10 albums before the band split up in 1969.[[129]](#cite_note-129) Another American [GI](/wiki/G.I._(military) "G.I. (military)"), [Jimmy James](/wiki/Jimmy_James_(singer) "Jimmy James (singer)"), born in Jamaica, moved to London after two local number one hits in 1960 with The Vagabonds, who built a strong reputation as a live act. They released a live album and their studio debut, *The New Religion,* in 1966 and achieved moderate success with a few singles before the original Vagabonds broke up in 1970.[[130]](#cite_note-130) White [blues rock](/wiki/Blues_rock "Blues rock") musician [Alexis Korner](/wiki/Alexis_Korner "Alexis Korner") formed new jazz rock band CCS in 1970.[[131]](#cite_note-131) Interest in the blues would influence major British rock musicians, including [Eric Clapton](/wiki/Eric_Clapton "Eric Clapton"), [Mick Taylor](/wiki/Mick_Taylor "Mick Taylor"), [Peter Green](/wiki/Peter_Green_(musician) "Peter Green (musician)"), and [John Mayall](/wiki/John_Mayall "John Mayall"), the groups Free and [Cream](/wiki/Cream_(band) "Cream (band)") adopted an interest in a wider range of rhythm and blues styles.[[127]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002BritishR&B-127)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Steve_Windwood_toy2%2C_Hangout_Music_Festival_2012.jpg/250px-Steve_Windwood_toy2%2C_Hangout_Music_Festival_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Steve_Windwood_toy2,_Hangout_Music_Festival_2012.jpg)

[Steve Winwood](/wiki/Steve_Winwood "Steve Winwood") is known for his influential R&B work with the [Spencer Davis Group](/wiki/Spencer_Davis_Group "Spencer Davis Group") and [Traffic](/wiki/Traffic_(band) "Traffic (band)").

The Rolling Stones became the second most popular UK band (after [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"))[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_30-132) and led the "[British Invasion](/wiki/British_Invasion "British Invasion")" of the US pop charts.[[127]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002BritishR&B-127) The Rolling Stones covered [Bobby Womack](/wiki/Bobby_Womack "Bobby Womack") & the Valentinos'[[133]](#cite_note-133) song "[It's All Over Now](/wiki/It%27s_All_Over_Now "It's All Over Now")", giving them their first UK number one in 1964.[[134]](#cite_note-rollingwithp137-134) Under the influence of blues and R&B, bands such as the Rolling Stones, the [Yardbirds](/wiki/The_Yardbirds "The Yardbirds"), and the Animals, and more jazz-influenced bands like the Graham Bond Organisation and [Zoot Money](/wiki/Zoot_Money "Zoot Money"), had blue-eyed soul albums.[[127]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002BritishR&B-127) White R&B musicians popular in the UK included [Steve Winwood](/wiki/Steve_Winwood "Steve Winwood"), Frankie Miller, Scott Walker & [the Walker Brothers](/wiki/The_Walker_Brothers "The Walker Brothers"), the Animals from Newcastle,[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_29,_track_3-135) the [Spencer Davis Group](/wiki/Spencer_Davis_Group "Spencer Davis Group"), and [Van Morrison](/wiki/Van_Morrison "Van Morrison") & [Them](/wiki/Them_(band) "Them (band)") from Belfast.[[127]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002BritishR&B-127) None of these bands exclusively played rhythm and blues, but it remained at the core of their early albums.[[127]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002BritishR&B-127)

[Champion Jack Dupree](/wiki/Champion_Jack_Dupree "Champion Jack Dupree") was a [New Orleans blues](/wiki/New_Orleans_blues "New Orleans blues") and [boogie woogie](/wiki/Boogie_woogie "Boogie woogie") pianist who toured Europe and settled there from 1960, living in Switzerland and Denmark, then in [Halifax](/wiki/Halifax,_West_Yorkshire "Halifax, West Yorkshire"), England, in the 1970s and 1980s, before finally settling in Germany.[[136]](#cite_note-136) [Average White Band](/wiki/Average_White_Band "Average White Band") and [Ian Dury](/wiki/Ian_Dury "Ian Dury") released funky singles. From the 1970s to 1980s, [Carl Douglas](/wiki/Carl_Douglas "Carl Douglas"), [Hot Chocolate](/wiki/Hot_Chocolate "Hot Chocolate"), Delegation, Junior, Central Line, Princess, Jaki Graham, [Loose Ends](/wiki/Loose_Ends_(band) "Loose Ends (band)"), the Pasadenas [Ruby Turner](/wiki/Ruby_Turner "Ruby Turner"), and [Soul II Soul](/wiki/Soul_II_Soul "Soul II Soul") gained hits on the pop or R&B charts.[[137]](#cite_note-137) UK R&B acts who gained success during the 1990s include [Mark Morrison](/wiki/Mark_Morrison "Mark Morrison"), [Eternal](/wiki/Eternal_(group) "Eternal (group)"), the Chimes, [Carleen Anderson](/wiki/Carleen_Anderson "Carleen Anderson"), [D'Influence](/wiki/D%27Influence "D'Influence"), Nu Colourz, Omar, and Bryan Powell.[[138]](#cite_note-138) The music of the British [mod subculture](/wiki/Mod_(subculture) "Mod (subculture)") grew out of rhythm and blues and later soul performed by artists who were not available to the small London clubs where the scene originated.[[139]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002Mod-139) In the late 1960s, [the Who](/wiki/The_Who "The Who") performed American R&B songs such as the Motown hit "[Heat Wave](/wiki/Heat_Wave_(1963_song) "Heat Wave (1963 song)")", a song which reflected the young mod lifestyle.[[139]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002Mod-139) Many of these bands enjoyed national success in the UK, but found it difficult to break into the American music market.[[139]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002Mod-139) The British white R&B bands produced music which was very different in tone from that of African-American artists.[[127]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002BritishR&B-127)
